origin_explanation: from Gk. paramonē through Chr.-Ar. barāmūn 'eve of a festival'
from doryämin, below; also Chr.-Ar. baryūmīn, baryūmīn, Syr. beryāmīn.

tabarza (K*), passive; tabäräza (K*M) 'evacuate the bowels, after evacuating the bowels'; tabäraz (T.M; perhaps rather tabäräz), raz (Lt) 'urine'; tabriz (K*) 'excrement, dregs, lees'; from Ar. (ta)bāraza 'evacuate the bowels' Amh. täbäraz 'urine'. See also tarbiz.

phonetics: (yəbəraz) (K*) 'dilute with water, mix two kinds of beverage together'
meaning: dilute with water, mix two kinds of beverage together
origin_explanation: tabaraza (K*), passive; tabäräza (K*) 'be mixed with one another'; baruz (K*) 'diluted'; barz (K*) 'honeyed water'. ETHIOPIC: Amh.Gur. barz 'honeyed water', Tna. Barzi, Har. birzi; also in Cushitic: Qab. barzi-ta, Qem. barz, Or. barzi. For barz, see Conti Rossini 1912:180. For G. mazar, below.
the consonant ž indicates that it is a loanword; probably corruption of Ar. zabarğad 'topaz', Persian origin (Hava 283).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäs'e 'pierce', Amh. bässa 'perfo rate, puncture'.

SEMITIC: Ar. (Magrib) m-basbäs 'drenched' (for which see D. Cohen 1976:72, under bšbš). ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäsbäsa 'become moist, become moldy', Tna. bäsbäsä 'be rotten', Amh. bäšäbäšä 'decay, be wet'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. bäsbäs 'be wet'.

phonetics: (K*) 'open wide (the eyes), wink, twinkle, move, shake, wag (the tail), overthrow'
meaning: open wide (the eyes), wink, twinkle, move, shake, wag (the tail), overthrow
origin_explanation: anbasbäsa 'leap, jump, skip, move quickly, run rapidly, go here and there, glitter, sparkle (eyes), flash light up, gleam, move the eyelids, wink, twitch, blink, palpitate, be shaken'; nabasbäs, näbäsbaš 'a leap, quick motion (such as the movement of the eyes or of the wind), blinking, twitch, tremulous movement'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tä)bäšäbäšä 'throw down one another'. For 'light up, move the eyelids, wag (the tail)', the G. root is related to Ar. bassa, basbasa 'show, open the eyes, wag the tail' (Ludolf 237, Dillmann 509). Conti 1980:78 compares Ar. basbasa 'run out (liquid)'. See also fsfs.
from Amh. bässäkä, same.

absala 'cook'; tabasala 'be cooked'; bəsul 'cooked, ripe'; bəslo, (Lt) baslä 'cooked food'; bäslat (Lt) 'decoction, mixture'; abäsäli (K*) 'cook' (n); mabsal, mabassal, mabsali 'cook' (n); mabsal, mabsal 'cooking pot'; (beta mabsäl 'kitchen, oven'); mabsälät 'dish, cooked dish'; tabsəl, täbsəl, see below. SEMITIC: Ar. (ra)bšala 'boil dates and dry them', Ar. (Dat) basal 'cooked meat', SAr. m-bs,l 'cooking place', Soq. behel 'be cooked' (with h corresponding to s), Šh. bešal, Heb. bāšal 'boil' (intr), Syr. bəšal, Aram. bəšal, Md. bšl, Akk. bašālu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäsälä 'be cooked', Te. bäsäla, Amh.Gaf. bäsälä, Har. bäsälä, Gur. bäsälä.

meaning: 'red onion'; combined of bisal (for baṣal) 'onion' (see baṣal, above) and of tumā 'garlic' (from Ar. tūm).
origin_explanation: combined of bisal (for baṣal) 'onion' (see baṣal, above) and of tumā 'garlic' (from Ar. tūm).

meaning: 'glistening white stone', lit. 'spittle of the moon'.
origin_explanation: From Ar. busūq, in busūq al-qamar 'glistening white stone', lit. 'spittle of the moon'.

SEMITIC: Ar. baṣara 'cut', Soq. bḍr 'rend asunder', Heb. bāṣar 'harvest a vintage, cut off', Aram.Syr. baṣṣār 'tear off'. The meanings 'lessen, lack, diminish, subdue' of bṣr (Heb.Syr.Aram.Md) are to be derived from bṣr 'cut' (so also, with reservations, Baumgartner 142, as against Koehler 142). ETHIOPIC: Amh. abāṭṭārā 'separate the good from the bad'. The root is related to baṣṣala 'tear, lacerate' (with alternance of liquids); see above.

meaning: 'be a companion'; denominative of his 'companion'; see bṣy I, II.

origin_explanation: From Heb. baṭ, or from Gk. batos going back to Hebrew; also in Te.Amh. bat. See also bāḍos.

meaning: 'hollow out, eat the inside, rend with the teeth, consume';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: a reduplicative root bṭṭ: Ar baṭṭa 'cut off', Heb. bāṭaṭ. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bāṭbāṭā 'gnaw, nibble', Amh. bāṭbāṭā. The root is related to bḍḍ (see baḍḍa), and bṭṭ (see baṭaṭa).

meaning: 'break, tear apart, burst, tear off, remove, displace, turn over (a big stone)';
origin_explanation: abṭaka, causative; also 'cause to settle a quarrel'. taṭabaka, pass. of baṭaka; also 'break' (intr); baṭuk, pass. part. of baṭaka.

meaning: 'broken thing, a cut, a slash, gap, animal that has been torn apart', 'fracture, breaking'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. baṭaka 'cut off'. ETHIOPIC: Te. bāṭka 'cut off', Tna. bāṭkā 'be torn', abṭākāṭkā 'tear to pieces', bāṭākā 'uproot, pull out', bōṭṭayhā 'cut of', passed into Cushitic: Qab. buṭukko 'cut', Bil. bāṭāk. The root is related to ftk, ftq, bdq, btq 'split, kill, slaughter': Ar. fataka, fataqa, Soq. bdq, Ar. badaka, Heb. biṭṭeq, Akk. baṭaqu; Amh. bāččāqā. See also badaqa, above.
From Ar. baṭūl 'virgin'. In other Semitic languages: Heb. bəṭūlā 'virgin', Aram.Syr. baṭūl-tā, Ug. bt-l, baṭūlū 'young man', baṭūl-tu 'adolescent nubile girl'.

SEMITIC: the root is related to Ar. baṭala 'separate, detach' (with alternance of liquids). ETHIOPIC: Tna. bāṭānā 'disperse, scatter', Amh. bāṭānā, Arg. bāṭānna, Har. bēṭāna; passed into Cushitic: Qua.Bil. bāṭān, Kham. biṭen, Qem. bāṭān.
SEMITIC: Ar. baṭara 'cut', Heb. bāṭar, Aram. biṭrā 'piece', Akk. buṭṭuru 'mutilate' (?). ETHIOPIC: Te. bāṭray 'deprived of everything', bāṭārā 'separate'. The root is related to matara.

meaning: 'branch, shoot, rod, scepter, stave, tribe, race, offspring'; (baṭra nagad 'tribe, race, offspring');
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. bāṭor 'stick', Tna. bāṭri, Amh. bāṭṭār, Arg. baṭṭor, Gur. bāṭṭor; with metathesis in Har. bāṭri, Gaf. bārṭā, Gur. bārṭ. For 'rod, scepter' and 'tribe', cp. Heb. šēbeṭ, maṭṭe(h), and Ar. 'aṣā (for which see Landberg 1909:1755).
see baḍr III.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. baṭerā 'invisible gas which is in the atmosphere' (probably from Geez).
see baṭrayārk, below.
from Gk. paterikon, acc.sg. of paterikos 'pertaining to the Fathers of the Church'.
from Gk. paterōn, gen. pl. of patēr 'father'.
from Gk. patriarkhēs 'patriarch'; also Amh. bāṭrāyrak, Tna. bāṭrayārk, baṭaryarak.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. bāṭṭāṭā 'wear out', buṭuṭṭo 'worn-out clothes', Har. buṭuṭṭu, Gur. buṭoṭo, Tna. 'grow old' (refers to a person, but could probably also be applied to a thing); also in Cushitic buṭuṭṭu 'worn-out cloth', Al. buṭuṭṭo, Mo. (see Leslau 1979:164, under buṭaṭo). See also ta.
from beta 'spend the night, stay', with reduplication of the last radical. For the root bṭṭ augment t, cp. also Aram. baṭaṭ 'stay, dwell'.

meaning: 'mix up by stirring vigorously (e.g. honey in water)';
origin_explanation: in view of Tna. bāṣbāṣā, with ṣ (but also bāṭbāṭā). G. baṭbaṭa is undoubtedly an Amharism from bāṭbāṭā. The original root is bṣbṣ; see baṣbaṣa, above.

meaning: 'make an incision, make gashes, scarify, scratch open, cut with a scalpel';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. bāṭḥā 'make an incision', Amh. bāṭṭa (from bṭḥ), b'aṭṭāṭā 'scratch, tear', Te. bālṭaḥa (augmented by l); passed into Cushitic: Bil. bālṭeḥ (from Tigre). Be. beḍḥa 'cupping'. See also baṭḥa.
from Ar. baṭṭīḥ, of Syriac origin (Fraenkel 140), also in Amh. bāṭṭiḥ. Ar. baṭṭiḥ passed into Som. barṭīḥ. The root also occurs in Heb. 'abaṭṭīḥ, Aram. 'abaṭṭīḥā. See also bāṭiq.
perhaps for baṭha 'cut, make an incision' (see above), the cutting suggesting a defect in the ear.

meaning: 'be useless, be idle, be futile, be void, fail, be without aim and reason, be of no effect, be annulled, be reduced to nothing, be invalid, be abolished, be in vain, come to nothing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. baṭala 'be vain, be useless, be invalid', SAR. bṭl-t 'uselessness', S.h. bṭol 'be reduced to nothing', Heb. bāṭal 'cease working', Syr. baṭel, Md. biṭil, Akk. baṭālu 'stop, cease, come to an end'. ETHIOPIC: Te. bāṭala 'be useless, be vain', bāṭāllā 'stop' (tr), Amh. bāṭṭālā 'be spoiled, be ruined, be abolished', Har. abāṭālā 'abolish'. Gur. baṭil 'unjust', and Har. bāṭil are taken from Arabic.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. bāṭṭālā 'be torn, be uprooted'. Note the rare form with o after the 1st radical.

meaning: corrupt form of basīlāwī (see below) through confusion with bīsōlīs 'Basil'.
related to Gk. basileus 'king'. The Melchites are so named because they adopted the dogmatic defini-tion of the court. On the Melchites, see Asfalg-Krüger 261. See also bisəlisat, above.
origin_explanation: related to Gk. basileus 'king'.

phonetics: (T.K) bisom 'balsam tree', (T) = Amh. bamba 'bao-bab, (KG) fig tree'
origin_explanation: from Heb. bōśem, beśem; note Te. bäsäm, from Ar. bašām. In the other Semitic languages: Aram. buśmā, Syr. hesmā, Ph. bśm. See also balasān, above.
see bisos, below.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäsnäwā 'bring out bad breath', Te. bäsna wäda 'belch', Amh. bäsänna 'have bad breath due to indigestion'.

phonetics: (T) basənsos 'the ninth month of the Cop-tic calendar (corresponds to G. gənboť)'
origin_explanation: from Coptic pašons through Chr.-Ar. bašans.
see bisos, below.

SEMETIC: Ar. baśśara 'announce good news', Sar. t-Soq. ibšir, Heb. biśśer, Aram. baśśar, baśśar (with metathesis), Ug. bśr 'good tidings', Akk. buššuru 'bring good news'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. 'abśārā 'announce good news', bäśśāra (from Arabic), Amh. abäśśärä 'announce good news', yämäśrač, exclamation on hearing good news (for the alternation b:m, see Cohen 1931:387-9); passed into Cushitic: Sa. basar 'announce good news', Be. basāra 'good news'.

from Heb. bāśār 'flesh' (so Dillmann 509 Praetorius 1879:14), while Har.Gur. bäsər, Gaf. bäsärä are cognates with the Semitic 'flesh': Ar. basar-at 'human skin, human flesh', SaR. bsr, Heb. bāśār 'flesh', Aram. bisrā, bisrā, Syr. besrā 'flesh, body', Ug. bśr 'flesh'.
from Ar. (Egypt) baṣṣa; also Amh. bäsäsä. The meaning 'hunt' would come from the meaning 'look and there'.

phonetics: (Lt) busus, basus, basīs 'cotton stuff, green linen, fine linen'
origin_explanation: from Gk. byssos 'byssus'; also Tna. biso 'green (leather, velvet)'. On the origin of Gk. byssos in connection with Heb. būṣ, Akk. būṣu, Aram.-Syr. būṣ, see Buhl 89, D. Cohen 1976:52, and Masson 22. For Ar. bisa(t), see Vollers 1897:295. For the Egyptian origin of Heb. būṣ, see T. Lam-din, JAOS 73 (1953).147-8. On the Sanskrit origin of this root, see Rabin 1968:436. See also bisən, bisənso.
from Ar. bisāt; also Amh. bast, Har. bisāt.
from Gk. stauros 'cross' through Coptic pestawros, pe being the article, through Arabic where a foreign b becomes b.

phonetics: (L.K) bad-a ñdo 'value, define, determine, esteem, evaluate, be fortunate, be blessed, be honored'
'declare or make blessed, proclaim bless-ed, become happy, become blessed, honor'
'be valued, be honored, be pro-claimed blessed'
'consider oneself blessed, blessed, make blessed, honor, call one to felicitate, give thanks, proclaim beatitude, esteem, admire'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäs'e 'be blessed', Te. bäs'a (with >), Amh. bäs'w 'blessed, His Holiness' (from Geez).

on the basis of Tna. bäśbäśä 'mix up by stirring putrid'. G. baśbāśa has perhaps also the meaning 'be putrid' and is to be compared with Ar. bassa 'ooze', Heb. bissā 'oozy place'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäśbäśä, see above, Amh. bäśbāṭä 'mix up by stirring, become turbid', Arg. bäṭbäṭä 'mix up by stirring, become turbid', Amh. bäṭbäṭä 'stir, dilute'. See also baṭbaṭa, below.

phonetics: (yəbśāh) (T) baśha 'arrive, happen, befall, come to pass, occur, fall to one's lot, appear (in court), reach, amount to, result in, pervade, extend, be accomplished, (K) reach a mature age'
causative; also 'bring, bring forward, deliver, lead, make ready, convey, demon-strate, explain, prove'
'come upon, be connected with, be associated with, bring before a judge, summon one another'
'one who arrives, one who is ready, matured'
'one who has reached the age of maturity, mature')
(Lt) mabśəh 'place of arrival, destina-tion, result (in arithmetic)'
(Gr.457) 'opportunity, that which oc-curs, that which happens'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Sar. wśh 'reach, come to' (Conti Rossini 1931:139; W. Müller 1983:285). ETHIOPIC: Te. bäśha 'arrive', Tna. bäśhe; Te. bäśih 'youth', that is, 'a person who has reached (bäśha) the age of maturity', Tna. bäśhi; passed into Cushitic: Bil. bäṭeḥ 'youth' (from Te. bäśih), Be. bedah 'come'.

phonetics: (T.M) boṣal (not bosal as in Guidi 1906:920) 'onion, (T) garlic'
origin_explanation: from Ar. baṣal; also Te. bäsäl. Dillmann 545 seems to connect it with bassala 'tear', below. In other Semitic languages: Sar. bṣl 'onion', Soq. biṣle, Heb. bāṣāl, Syr. beṣlā, Aram. buṣlā, Ph. bṣl.

phonetics: 'tear, lacerate, cut, separate, strip, peel, dissolve'

origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. bassaļa 'strip of clothes or posses-sions', Sar. bṣl 'torn' (see M. Höfner, in Baum-gartner 1967:108), Aram. baṣṣal 'split, peel'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäsṣäl 'stub, cut piece', Amh. bäṭ-ṭälä 'be torn, be uprooted'. The root is related to fṣl: Ar. faṣala 'split', Heb. pīṣṣel 'bark, peel', Aram. paṣṣel, Syr. paṣal, Md. pṣl; Te. fänṣäla 'separate', Tna. fänṣälä (with augmented n). See also basara, below.

meaning: 'flatten' related to Ar. (ta)baṭa 'lie on the stomach', and (yin)baṭaḥa 'lie prone'. Heb. bāṭaḥ 'lie flat on one's belly'. See on Root Driver 1957:59, Kopf 7ff., Barr 90ff. and in Baumgartner 1967:225ff.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. baṭaṭa 'flatten', related to Ar. (ta)baṭa 'lie on the stomach', and (yin)baṭaḥa 'lie prone'. Heb. bāṭaḥ 'lie flat on one's belly'. See on Root Driver 1957:59, Kopf 7ff., Barr 90ff. and in Baumgartner 1967:225ff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. baṭṭā bālā 'lie down, be stretched out', Te. baṭṭa bela, Amh. bāṭṭāṭā 'stretch out' in Cushitic: Bil. baṭ y 'lie down, stretch out'. Sa. baṭaḥ.
From Ar. buṭm (from Aramaic; see Fraenkel 139), Low 68-70. Akk. buṭnu. For m:n in the various languages, see Růžička 128.
see baṭṭiḥ.
probably from Amh. abāṭṭārā 'winnow'; also in Arg. abeṭṭārā, Gaf.Gur. abiṭṭārā.
From Gk. patrikios, fem. patrikia 'patrician' (of Latin [patricius] origin) through Ar. baṭrīq, baṭrīqa.
From Ar. baṭrasīn, baṭraṣīl < Gk. epitrakhēlion 'stole' (Vollers 1897:295).
From Lat. patricius through Gk. patrikios; note, however the enigmatic ending -ṭā of G. baṭriṭā.

SEMITIC: Ar. baṭaša 'hit, strike, behave in a brutal way', Aram. baṭaš 'stamp'. Syr. buṭšāṭā 'striking with the heels', Md. bṭš 'tread down, trample, treat violently'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. bāṭṭāšā 'break off by pulling', Gaf. biṭṭāšā. Arg. beṭṭāšā, Gur. bāṭāšā, Har. bāṭāša.
From Gk. batos 'bramble bush'; also Amh. baṭos, Ar. bāṭūs. See also ṭaṭos.

SEMITIC: Ar. baṭaṭa 'flatten' related to Ar. (ta)baṭa 'lie on the stomach', and (yin)baṭaḥa 'lie prone'. Heb. bāṭaḥ 'lie flat on one's belly'. See on Root Driver 1957:59, Kopf 7ff., Barr 90ff. and in Baumgartner 1967:225ff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. baṭṭā bālā 'lie down, be stretched out', Te. baṭṭa bela, Amh. bāṭṭāṭā 'stretch out' in Cushitic: Bil. baṭ y 'lie down, stretch out'. Sa. baṭaḥ.
ETHIOPIC: Te. beṭṭa bela (in Reinisch 1902:71) Amh. bāṭṭāṭā, baṭṭ alā; also in Cushitic: Som. bōd 'a jump'.
SEMITIC: Ar. (Syria) baṭbaṭ 'defecate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. bāṭṭāṭ bela, Amh. bāṭṭāṭā.

meaning: 'enter, penetrate, proceed, frequent, be involved, intermingle, have intercourse';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. bāɔa 'return' (also 'come' according to Noeldekke, ZDMG 40[1886].726), SAR. bh 'enter'. Heb. bā 'come', Ug. bɔa, Ph. bɔ, Akk. bā 'come alongside, come in'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. boɔe 'enter', Te. boɔa, Har. probably also Gur. bɔɔā 'go down to a lower place'. For G. mabāɔ, cp. Amh. māba (from For G. baɔat, cp. Amh. bāɔat 'entering, entrance', baɔata 'presentation of the Virgin in the temple', lit. 'her entering' (from Geez), Tna. baɔata. For baɔatt 'cave', cp. Te. bāɔat, bāɔat, bāɔatti, baḥɔata. For the denominative baɔata, see above. The root passed into Cushitic: Be. biɔ 'return home'.
SEMITIC: Ar. bāḥa (bwh) 'be revealed, become public'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. bāḥa (KBT) 'be visible, be seen' (from Geez), buh (DTW) 'that which is seen'.
SEMITIC: Ar. (ɔa)bāḥa (bwh) 'permit'. See also bḥt II.
from Ar. buq 'trumpet', considered to be of Latin (buccina) origin; also late Gk. būkīnē.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. bozā 'be dull, be stupid', Gur. buz 'ignorant, stupid', Har. bāzāy 'idle'.
from Ar. abyad. See also badā, and bys III.

meaning: the seventh month of the year;
origin_explanation: from Ar. bayādir, pl. of baydar 'threshing floor', zamānu-l-baydar 'threshing season'.

meaning: 'discern, distinguish, remark, pay attention, notice, recognize, consider, demonstrate, decide, (Y.T) judge, pass judgment, expound';
origin_explanation: The basic meaning is that of 'separate, distinguish > discern, know'. SEMITIC: Ar. bāna 'be separated, be clear, be evident', Sar. byn 'separate, know' (Müller 1963:307), Heb. (byn), he-bīn 'understand', Syr. bayyen 'show, discern', Md. ban 'separate', Ug. byn 'know'.

meaning: 'become evident, become clear', Tna. bäyyänä 'discern, decide', Amh. bäyyänä 'distinguish, give a verdict'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. bäyän 'decide'.
origin_explanation: Te. (ta)bäyyäna 'become evident, become clear', Tna. bäyyänä 'discern, decide', Amh. bäyyänä 'distinguish, give a verdict'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. bäyän 'decide'.

meaning: 'between'; əmbayna, əmbayna, see above; babayna 'for, because of, for the sake of, on account of'; babayna zä 'because of this, therefore, about this'; babayna mənt 'why?'; babayna-, babaynäti- (with suffix pronouns) 'between, among, to one another'; babaynom 'between them, together'.
origin_explanation: from the basic meaning of bayyana 'separate' (see above). For more details, see Dillmann 1907:374, 389-90, 403. SEMITIC: Ar. bayn 'interval', bayna 'between', Sar. byn 'between', Heb. ben, Aram. ben, be, Syr. bayna(y), Md. binat-, binia, Ph.Ug. bn. ETHIOPIC: Te. bäyn 'separate' (adj), əb bäyn 'between', bän- (with suffix pronouns) 'alone, by oneself', Tna. bäyn- (with suffix pronouns) 'alone, apart'. For Te. bäyn, see Rundgren, OS 16 (1961). 134-6.

meaning: (K*T.M) 'separate, cut in equal parts, choose'; bayyasa 'separate, distinguish, discern, mark out'; tabayasa (K*), passive of besa; bayyasi, act. part. of bayyasa; bayyus (K*) 'separated'; bis (pl. abyas) 'single, individual, a certain piece, some, friend, fellow, companion, comrade, equal, neighbor, intimate'; (bis labisu 'one to another'); bisa (Gr.428) 'near, at the side of, beside'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 539, and Praetorius 1890:30 compare the Geez root with bs', bd' 'cut'. For bys, bsw 'cut, separate', see D. Cohen 1976:76, under bs/d. The Geez root is perhaps related to bsy >

meaning: become companions (to one another), become friends, become equal'; astabäyäsa, causative; tabäyäsi (one would expect tabäyäsi) yäsi (M) 'companion'; mastabäyäs 'companion, participant'; denominative of bis (see bys I). For the denominative, cp. also Amh. täbäyyätä 'become friends'. For another form of the G. denominative, see above.
origin_explanation: denominative of bis (see bys I). For the denominative, cp. also Amh. täbäyyätä 'become friends'. For another form of the G. denominative, see above.

SEMITIC: Ar. bäda (byd) 'grow yellow', bayyada 'make white'. Dietrich 294 compares Akk. 'white'. ETHIOPIC: Te. bäyyäsa 'be bright, be brilliant', Amh. bačča 'yellow'; also in Cushitic: Kham. bečča 'be yellow'. For the relation between 'white' and 'yellow', cp. Amh. näṭṭa 'become white, pale, turn (cereal)'. The root bys 'become white' is the origin of the noun 'egg' in the various Semitic languages: Ar. bayda, Heb. beṣa. F. Rosenthal (private communication) prefers to derive Ar. bäda 'be white' from bayda 'egg'.

meaning: spend the night, remain overnight, remain, stay in a house, dwell' (probably denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. bayt 'house', Sar. byt, Soq. beyt, Heb. bayit, Aram.Syr. bayta, Ug.Ph. bt, Akk. bitu. For 'spend the night', cp. Ar. bāta, Sar. byt, Aram.Syr. bāt, Md. but, Ug. bt, Akk. bātu. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh. bet 'house'; Te. beta 'spend the night'. See also betata.
it is perhaps connected with byt I and the causing to kneel would then refer to the cattle that spend the night in their respective places while resting or kneeling.
From Cushitic: Al. bez-ečču 'morning star', Bil. (with alternance d:z). Qem. bez 'make clear'.

meaning: 'ransom, redemption, propitiation, substitute, (Lt) expiation; in exchange for, for the sake of'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. boz 'harp string'; is there any connection with Te. boza 'a certain tune of the flute played at games'?

SEMITIC: Ar. bazza, bazbaza 'wrest, snatch', Heb. bazaz 'take spoils', Aram. bazaz, bazbez 'pillage', Syr. Md. baz. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäzbäzä 'pillage', Te. bäzbäza, bäzäbbäzä, Gur. bäzäbbäzä; passed into Cushitic: Qem. bäzäbäzä.
SEMITIC: PbH. bizbez 'waste', Aram. bazbez. The bazbaza is perhaps a transcription of the Hebrew verb.
SEMITIC: Ar. bazā (bzw) 'subdue, consider oneself superior to another', Heb. bāzā (bzy), bāz 'despise', Aram. bazā, basā, Akk. buzzuwu 'treat unjustly'. See also bzz (bazza).

meaning: (T) 'be numerous, be abundant, be much, be many, increase' (intr); 'abəzəha 'multiply, make numerous, increase' (tr), 'augment, produce a lot of, have a lot of'; tabəzəha 'increase, multiply' (intr); tabäzəha 'be multiplied, multiply together'; 'astabäzəha, caus. of. of tabäzəha; bəzuh 'many, much, numerous, abundant'; (bəzuha, babəzuh 'much, in large numbers'); bəzəh 'multitude, large number, large amount'; bəzhat 'multitude'; 'abəzəhi, 'abəzəhi, part. of 'abəzəha; mabzəhi, same; mabzəht, mabzəht 'major part, majority of, most of the most, large part, the greatest part, multitude'; (mabzəhta, mabzəhto 'often, frequently, most of it').
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. bazh 'abundance of wealth'. The connection with Ar. bādih 'high, proud' suggested by Noeldeke, ZDMG 66 (1912).788 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. bäzhe 'be abundant, be numerous', Te.Har. bäzäha, Arg. bäzzaha, Amh.Gur. bäzza; passed into Cushitic: Bil. bäggag 'be numerous', Kham. biqeq, Qua. bägağ.
perhaps from Gk. mousikē (Lat. musica); also Tna. bäzeqa 'music, harmonium', Amh. maziqa.
from Ar. zibāq, zābiq, of Persian (ziva) origin.
it renders Heb. bäzāq, translated 'lightning'. For Heb. bäzāq, cp. Jud.-Aram. bəzaq (J. Blau, VT 1956].97).

meaning: 'belt, girdle of fiber worn by monks'.

origin_explanation: from Amh. bazra; also Te.Tna.Gur.Arg. bazra; also in Cushitic: Sid. bāzra, Bil. badra (pl. bazir), Af. badira.

from Ar. badr, bazr. For the root in Semitic, cp. Sh. bedər 'seed', Heb. bāzar 'scatter', Aram. bizrā 'seed', Syr. baḏar 'strew, disperse'; related to pzr: Heb. pizzer 'scatter'. See also Wagner 33, under bzr.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. bāzzātā, bozzātā 'gin cotton', Te. bāzzātā, Amh. bazzət 'wool'.

SEMITIC: Ar. bāza (byz) 'be saved, live'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. beza, bāğa 'ransom' (n), Te. beza 'redeem', Amh. bāzā, bezzā 'ransom, rescue, save', beza 'ransom' (n). The connection between bezawa and bazbaza 'pillage' suggested by Praetorius 1890:33 is doubtful. Since many verbs ending in -wa are denominatives, it is possible that bezawa is a denominative from bezā.

meaning: = Amh. sābāz 'kind of plant from which one makes baskets'
